title: Biocompatibility of nickel-titanium shape memory metal and its corrosion behavior in human cell cultures

This is also presumably why sensitization and immune
disorders from metallic prostheses are very unusual,
although, for example, the stainless steel used for im-
plants contains perceptible amounts of nickel.28,29
Titanium is the other main component of Nitinol. It
was chosen as one of the experimental materials be-
cause it has been widely used in traumatology and
orthopedics. It is known to be well tolerated by nonos-
seous tissue and it forms a close connection with
The composite material is known to have shown
some toxicity in in vitro studies. It is used as a tooth-
ﬁlling material in dentistry.32 In this study, it was cho-

The corrosion properties of metals depend on the
surface reaction taking place in the respective environ-
Our study showed that ﬁbroblasts appear to be more
sensitive to the growth-inhibiting effect of composite
ments. In many practical applications, corrosion resis-
tance is due to passivation of the metal surface, i.e.,
material and white soft parafﬁn or the growth stimula-
tion of titanium than osteoblasts. The responses of both
the metal atoms at the surface react with the oxygen
(and nitrogen) present in the environment to form a

stable, nonreactive surface layer of oxide. This in situ

thopedic use were tested with both ﬁbroblasts and os-
presence of oxygen, rapid self-healing takes place if
the surface ﬁlm is mechanically damaged.37,38

It may thus be that after some initial dissolution of
